summary: Backend security regression: verified SEC-001 (JWT secret rotation — all 3 logins still work, protected /driver/me + /shipper/me accept new tokens, reject missing/garbage), SEC-002 (POST /api/orders/{id}/advance now returns 401 anonymous, 401 with bad token, 403 with non-assigned shipper/admin token; reject also requires auth; the legitimately assigned demo driver can still advance the lifecycle all the way to 'delivered'), SEC-003 (POST /api/payments/webhook returns 400 for both no-signature and bad-signature payloads — no order mutation possible because the 400 fires before any DB write). Full regression of accept -> advance -> delivered with the demo driver token succeeded. Admin-only endpoints (/admin/manage/drivers, /admin/pricing) return 401 without token, 403 with a driver token, and 200 with an admin token. 20 passed, 1 skipped (skipped test simply couldn't enumerate a shipper order because the available shipper list endpoint here is /api/shipper/shipments, not /shipper/orders — does not weaken SEC-003 verification).
critical_code_review_comments: advance_order correctly enforces driver_id ownership check (orders.py L515-516) AFTER get_current_driver_id auth — good.
reject_order now also requires authenticated driver via get_current_driver_id (orders.py L483) — good.

stripe_webhook explicitly rejects when STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET is empty (payments.py L355-360) raising 400 BEFORE any DB access — secure fail-closed behavior. Verified no order mutation possible in this mode.
JWT_SECRET in backend/.env is a strong 64-char random value — rotation confirmed via successful login + protected-route round trip.
seed_data_creation: Called POST /api/seed-demo once at suite startup (idempotent). Used POST /api/orders/add-pending to ensure a pending order exists for accept->advance regression.
context_for_next_testing_agent: Use EXPO_PUBLIC_BACKEND_URL (not EXPO_BACKEND_URL) from /app/frontend/.env. Demo creds in /app/memory/test_credentials.md. The Stripe webhook secret is intentionally empty in this env, so ALL /payments/webhook calls should return 400 — that is the documented secure-by-default behavior, not a regression. The order state machine progression from 'accepted' goes: accepted -> enroute_pickup -> arrived_pickup -> picked_up -> enroute_dropoff -> arrived_dropoff -> delivered. POSTing /orders/{id}/advance with an empty body lets the server auto-resolve the next state — preferred over passing explicit next_status.
